examples.title: Basic Shell Interpretive Cycle
examples.description: Pseudo-code showing simplified shell REPL loop
examples.code:
PROCEDURE shell_interpretive_cycle
 WHILE true DO
 PRINT prompt
 command_line ← READ_LINE() // Read input
 IF command_line = "exit" THEN
 BREAK // Exit loop
 END IF
 tokens ← TOKENIZE(command_line) // Parse into tokens
 parsed_cmd ← PARSE(tokens) // Build command structure
 expanded_cmd ← EXPAND(parsed_cmd) // Variable/command substitution
 setup_redirection(expanded_cmd) // Handle I/O redirection
 IF has_pipe THEN
 setup_pipeline(expanded_cmd) // Connect commands with pipes
 END IF
 IF background THEN
 fork_and_exec_bg(expanded_cmd) // Run in background
 ELSE
 fork_and_exec_fg(expanded_cmd) // Run in foreground, wait
 END IF
 display_output() // Print results
 END WHILE
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the basic REPL loop of a shell: continuously reading commands, parsing them, setting up I/O, executing, and displaying output
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is command length

examples.title: Tokenization Function
examples.description: Algorithm to break command line into tokens
examples.code:
FUNCTION tokenize(input_string)
 tokens ← EMPTY_LIST
 current_token ← ""
 i ← 0
 WHILE i < length(input_string) DO
 char ← input_string[i]
 IF char is whitespace THEN
 IF current_token ≠ "" THEN
 ADD current_token TO tokens
 current_token ← ""
 END IF
 ELSE IF char is metacharacter THEN
 // Handle metacharacters like |, >, <, ;
 IF current_token ≠ "" THEN
 ADD current_token TO tokens
 current_token ← ""
 END IF
 ADD char TO tokens
 ELSE
 current_token ← current_token + char
 END IF
 i ← i + 1
 END WHILE
 IF current_token ≠ "" THEN
 ADD current_token TO tokens
 END IF
 RETURN tokens
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Tokenization breaks the input string into separate tokens based on whitespace and metacharacters

examples.title: Fork-Exec Pattern for Command Execution
examples.description: How shell creates child process and executes command
examples.code:
FUNCTION execute_command(cmd)
 pid ← fork()
 IF pid < 0 THEN
 PRINT "Fork failed"
 RETURN ERROR
 ELSE IF pid == 0 THEN
 // This is child process
 setup_redirects(cmd.redirects)
 setup_pipes(cmd.pipes)
 // Replace this process with new program
 IF cmd.is_builtin THEN
 execute_builtin(cmd)
 ELSE
 execvp(cmd.name, cmd.args)
 // If exec returns, it failed
 PRINT "Command not found"
 EXIT(127)
 END IF
 ELSE
 // This is parent process
 IF cmd.run_in_background THEN
 ADD (pid, cmd) TO background_jobs
 RETURN
 ELSE
 waitpid(pid, &status, 0) // Wait for child
 RETURN status
 END IF
 END IF
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: The fork-exec pattern is fundamental to Unix process creation. fork() duplicates the process, and exec() replaces it with a new program
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for fork, O(n) for exec

examples.title: Pipeline Setup Example
examples.description: How shell sets up pipeline connecting multiple commands
examples.code:
# Pipeline: cat file.txt | grep 'error' | sort | uniq
# Shell creates pipes and connects them:

# Create pipe file descriptors
pipe1 = pipe() # [read_fd1, write_fd1]
pipe2 = pipe() # [read_fd2, write_fd2]
pipe3 = pipe() # [read_fd3, write_fd3]

# Fork for 'cat'
if (fork() == 0) {
 close(pipe1[0]) // Close read end
 dup2(pipe1[1], STDOUT) // Redirect stdout to pipe write
 close(pipe1[1])
 exec("cat", ["cat", "file.txt"])
}

# Fork for 'grep'
if (fork() == 0) {
 close(pipe1[1]) // Close write end of pipe1
 dup2(pipe1[0], STDIN) // Redirect stdin from pipe1 read
 close(pipe1[0])
 close(pipe2[0])
 dup2(pipe2[1], STDOUT) // Redirect stdout to pipe2
 close(pipe2[1])
 exec("grep", ["grep", "error"])
}

# Similar for 'sort' and 'uniq'...
# Parent closes all pipe ends and waits for last command
examples.explanation: Each command in a pipeline runs as a separate process, connected by pipes where stdout of one becomes stdin of the next
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n commands in pipeline
